fund.name: Nippon India Large Cap Fund - Direct Plan Growth Plan - Growth Option

allocations.rationale: Steady large-cap stocks with long-term consistency. This fund has been around for 18 years and has consistently delivered good returns without too much price swings.

fund.name: Motilal Oswal Large and Midcap Fund - Direct Plan Growth

allocations.rationale: A mix of large and mid-cap companies. Mid-caps can grow faster, but they're also a bit riskier. This fund has a good balance and has delivered strong returns over the last 6 years.

fund.name: HDFC Flexi Cap Fund - Growth Option - Direct Plan

allocations.rationale: This fund manager invests in companies of any size based on what looks best. It has been a leader for many years and works well for a flexible, long-term approach.

fund.name: Motilal Oswal Midcap Fund-Direct Plan-Growth Option

allocations.rationale: Mid-cap funds can give higher returns over 15+ years, but they're more volatile. Since you're young (35) with a 37-year horizon, this fits your aggressive appetite.

fund.name: Nippon India Small Cap Fund - Direct Plan Growth Plan - Growth Option

allocations.rationale: Small-cap stocks are the riskiest but can give the highest returns over the long run. With 37 years ahead, this small portion helps boost your overall growth.

fund.name: UTI Long Term Advantage Fund Series V - Direct Plan - Growth Option

allocations.rationale: An ELSS fund for tax-saving. It gives you an 80C deduction (up to ₹1.5L/year) while spending on stocks. Has a 3-year lock-in, then taxed at lower rates.

fund.name: Franklin India SHORT TERM INCOME PLAN - Direct - GROWTH

allocations.rationale: A safer fund for your emergency buffer and short-term goals like the child's education in 4 years. It invests in short-term bonds and debt, so values don't jump around much.

plan.setup_phase: Step 1: Open a direct mutual fund account on an app like Groww, Kuvera, Coin, or MFU. Step 2: Add your bank account and link it. Step 3: Start SIPs in each of the 7 funds above with the amounts mentioned. Step 4: Use your existing ₹2 lakh corpus: put ₹1.4 lakh into the ULIP handling your child's education goal (the short-term debt fund) and ₹60,000 into the large-cap fund for the home loan goal.
review_checkpoints: After 6 months: Make sure all SIPs are running correctly. Fix any failed payments immediately.
After 1 year: Compare each fund's returns to its category average. If one is trailing badly for a second year, consider switching.
Every year (annual review): Check your asset mix. If stocks have grown so much that equity is now 60% instead of 55%, move some money to the debt fund to get back to your target plan.
plan.rebalancing_guidelines: Once a year (say, in March), check if your portfolio has drifted. If any one category (like small-cap) has grown much more than planned, sell a little and buy a bit of the underperforming category to get back to your target mix. This keeps your risk in check.
plan.rationale: Your plan balances your aggressive appetite with your specific goals. You have 37 years to grow wealth, so we've put about 75% of your SIPs in direct stocks (large, mid, and small-cap funds). The remaining 25% goes to safer options like ELSS and short-term debt. Because you're 35 and have decades ahead, we can afford to ride out the ups and downs. The ELSS fund also helps you save tax while investing.
risks: Stock markets can drop 20-30% in a bad year — your portfolio value will temporarily go down. This is normal and recovers over time.
Small-cap and mid-cap funds are the riskiest part of your portfolio. They can have big swings in short periods.
Inflation can eat away at your purchasing power. That's why we keep a lot in equities.
Your debt fund for the child's education is sensitive to interest rate changes, but it's designed to be safer than stocks.
plan.disclaimer: For research/educational purposes only. Not certified financial advice.

sip_step_up.description: Increase SIP by 10% every year to match salary growth and inflation

aps.reasoning: The plan scores low on passivity (0.0) because it utilizes exclusively actively managed mutual funds (Large Cap, Mid Cap, Small Cap, Flexi Cap, ELSS), with no index funds. The turnover (0.6) is moderate due to an 'annual review' strategy that includes tactical switching if a fund trails significantly, though the core allocation is static. Cost emphasis (0.2) is low; while switching themes include cost, expense ratios are not the primary driver. Research vs cost (0.4) leans slightly toward research as it recommends specific fund managers, but lacks deep fundamental screening details. Time horizon (0.95) is very long-term, citing a 37-year horizon and decades-long compounding. Portfolio activeness (0.1) is very high (i.e., very active) because the portfolio is concentrated in funds with unmanaged stock-picking mandates rather than cap-weighted indices.

pqs.reasoning: The plan generally aligns well with the investor's aggressive profile and long horizon, achieving high scores on diversification (good mix of cap sizes and funds), risk-return appropriateness (equity-heavy appropriate for age/risk), and tax efficiency (correctly leveraging ELSS for a 20% taxpayer and filling 80C). Goal alignment is slightly lower because the home loan goal is 10 years away, yet the debt portion assigned to it (Short Term Debt) is too short for a 10-year horizon (usually 3-5 years), and theAllocation schedule at Year 10 suggests 28% debt, which contradicts the heavy equity bias needed to hit the 8Cr retirement goal given the current allocation. Internal consistency is penalized due to a critical contradiction: the text sets up a ULIP/ULIP-sounding product for the child's 4-year goal, but the portfolio allocation list contains only mutual funds and no ULIPs, creating a disconnect between the strategy description and the execution plan.
